text: The surgeon prepares for a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a bulky uterus approximately 15 centimeters in size. A supraumbilical port is placed, and a small 2-millimeter stab incision is made to lift the abdominal wall for the introduction of the Veress needle.
The surgeon performs irrigation and suction followed by the hanging drop test to confirm the placement of the Veress needle inside the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon attaches the tubing of the insufflator to the varus needle and begins the insufflation of CO₂ at a flow rate of one liter per minute, with the insufflator preset pressure set at fifteen millimeters of mercury.

The insufflator displays the actual pressure of CO₂ insufflation in the abdominal cavity, initially at ten millimeters of mercury, gradually increasing to the preset pressure of fifteen millimeters of mercury.
The surgeon removes the Veress needle and enlarges the incision to 11 mm, preparing for the insertion of a larger port.
The surgeon introduces the optical port into the abdominal cavity following the insertion of the varus needle and establishment of pneumoperitoneum.

The surgeon connects the gas and applies the baseball diamond concept for port placement. The first arc is positioned 18 centimeters from the uterine artery, and the second arc is 24 centimeters away, with a telescope placed at the second arc. Two trocars are inserted 7.5 centimeters lateral to the midline, creating two ipsilateral ports.

The surgeon introduces a contralateral port on the opposite side for the assistant. After all ports are established, a uterine manipulator is inserted, and the cervix is dilated using a number five Hegar dilator.
The surgeon holds the cervix with a Valsalum forceps on the anterior leaf and dilates it using a Hager dilator up to size five, preparing for the insertion of the uterine manipulator.
The surgeon introduces a uterine sound into the cervix, which has been previously dilated, as part of the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a speculum to further dilate the cervix, preparing for the insertion of surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses a number five Hager dilator to further dilate the cervix, followed by the insertion of a Mangeshkar uterine manipulator equipped with a 3.5 cm colpotomizer.

After introducing the uterine manipulator, the surgeon begins the hysterectomy by applying contralateral traction and coagulating the round ligament. The uterus appears bulky due to a right wall fibroid and multiple intramural fibroids.

The surgeon begins by coagulating the round ligament, followed by the fallopian tube and ovarian ligament. The procedure continues with the application of a ligature over the broad ligament, measuring 2.5 centimeters.
The surgeon separates the right adnexa, followed by applying contralateral traction to coagulate and dissect the round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovarian ligament on the left side.
In the surgical field, the large uterus is visible with limited space. The left side shows the sigmoid colon below, and the ovarian ligament is identified nearby.

The surgeon retroverts the uterus and opens the anterior leaf of the broad ligament. The left hand graspers stretch the peritoneum, allowing the harmonic scalpel to create a clear areolar tissue plane for separation.
The assistant rotates the uterus from the five o'clock to seven o'clock position while the surgeon continues to separate the anatomical structures.
The surgeon opens the midline uterovesical fold and continues the dissection towards the right side, maintaining the surgical approach.
The surgeon lifts the bladder and pushes the uterus towards the seven o'clock position, preparing to dissect the right side of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament.
The uterine artery is visible, and the uterus is positioned at the six o'clock position. The surgeon begins to separate the bladder from the uterus using surgical instruments.
The shiny vaginal fascia is exposed, and the surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate the bladder from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon moves laterally to the bladder pillar, performing lateralization to separate the bladder from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate the bladder from surrounding tissues, clearly identifying the bladder structure in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the posterior peritoneum, carefully separating it up to the arc of the uterosacral ligament, while managing the presence of a large fibroid in the posterior wall.

The surgeon uses a grasper to lift the uterus due to non-cooperation of the uterine manipulator. The light cable of the thirty-degree telescope is positioned at six o'clock, providing a view of the posterior cul-de-sac where the posterior peritoneum is being separated.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine artery without cutting, focusing first on one side before moving to the other. The right side of the posterior peritoneum is visibly separated using a ligature, with harmonic devices as an alternative option.
The surgeon separates the posterior peritoneum, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the uterine artery on the right side, using surgical instruments to carefully manage the vessel.
The surgeon reflects the remaining posterior peritoneum, making it easier to hold the uterus. The uterine artery is then coagulated at two distinct locations.
The surgeon divides the uterine structure, cutting through the right side of the uterus after coagulation.
The surgeon moves to the left side of the uterine structure and begins the division process, using surgical instruments to separate the tissue.
So it is done.

The surgeon begins the colpotomy procedure, using the tip of the harmonic device on the left side over the colpotomizer. The anterior colpotomy is initiated, with options to use a hook or spatula for the dissection.
The surgeon extends the anterior colpotomy from the left lateral side towards the right, using the harmonic device carefully to avoid damaging the colpotomizer tip.
If you will press hard your colpotomizer will be damaged.
So you should brush it without much hard pressure.
The surgeon performs the right side colpotomy by maneuvering the harmonic scalpel through the right port, completing the incision around the colpotomizer.
The surgeon completes the posterior colpotomy, with half of the incision made from the right side and the remaining half from the left side, using the harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon completes the colpotomy and pulls the uterus into the vagina. Due to its large size, the uterus is bisected for removal.

The surgeon uses a #1 Vicryl suture to take a bite on the uterosacral ligament along with the posterior vaginal vault. The suturing continues from right to left, with careful manipulation to push the bladder posteriorly to avoid pricking it. The knot is not tied at this stage.

We will tie the knot once we will return back Full thickness of the Alphasia will be taken And only three bite is required And then again we will return back in between So total six bite will be taken And now this is the
The surgeon takes a bite on the left corner, incorporating the utero sacral ligament and then proceeds to the anterior fornix.
The surgeon locks the sutures on the anterior vaginal vault, securing the tissue in place.

The assistant holds the other end of the suture tail while the surgeon returns to take three bites in between the first, applying cranial traction to keep the bladder away. Each suture is locked during this process.

The surgeon performs interlocking sutures for wall closure, demonstrating a technique that involves returning sutures. Various knotting methods such as extra corporeal square knot or dandy jamming knot may be used, but interlocking is preferred in this procedure.
The surgeon interlocks the suture and terminates it using the same tail, completing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon manipulates a long suture tail into a C shape and tightens a surgeon's knot to terminate the suture.
The surgeon completes the closure of the anterior vaginal valve, cuts the suture, and pulls the suture tail through a five-millimeter port. The cannula is then removed to extract the needle.
The surgeon performs suction irrigation in the surgical field, clearing debris and fluid to improve visibility.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for any signs of bleeding, confirming that there is no bleeding present. Fluid is then aspirated from the posterior cul-de-sac using suction.
